name: Johnny Hartman
abstract: John Maurice Hartman (July 3, 1923 – September 15, 1983) was an American jazz singer, known for his rich baritone voice and recordings of ballads. He sang and recorded with Earl Hines' and Dizzy Gillespie's big bands and with Erroll Garner.

works.t: Annie Get Your Gun: They Say That Falling in Love is Wonderful

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Johnny Hartman way before McCoy and Trane. Johnny Hartman wouldn’t leave home without me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Well that album, John Coltrane, Johnny Hartman... [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah, that’s a great album. I really enjoyed it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Johnny Hartman was a singer in that band. ... Johnny Hartman was our singer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Pizzarelli, Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: And me and Johnny Hartman, not together, but for Stash Records... Johnny Hartman gets sick. He’s supposed to make the record... Bernie Brightman calls and says, “I’ve got studio time on Wednesday. So why don’t you bring him in and we’ll make the record right away.”
about_quotes.doc_title: John Pizzarelli, Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Rudy Van Gelder
about_quotes.evidence: And there were different sections from the ballads and the Johnny Hartman sessions. ... That was two-track, that was vocal. It’s beautiful, I love that, its one of my favorite records.
about_quotes.doc_title: Rudy Van Gelder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Woody Herman
about_quotes.evidence: One, I'm very proud to say, with Johnny Hartman, which I feel will be the best exposure he's had in a long time.
